topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon sun beat down on Clara’s porch, making the air shimmer. She was retrieving the morning’s mail, a routine chore she usually approached with a degree of practiced indifference. Then, nestled between a utility bill and a glossy flyer, she saw it: a thick, cream-colored card, entirely blank save for a small, elegant script at the bottom that read, "Happy Birthday." Clara’s hand froze mid-air, the paper crinkling under her grip. A sudden, hot flush crept up her neck, turning her cheeks a deep crimson. She quickly shoved the card deep into her jeans pocket, her fingers trembling slightly as she walked back inside. Every movement felt exaggerated, a performance under scrutiny. She spent the rest of the day meticulously rearranging books on her shelf, trying to anchor herself against the strange, buzzing feeling that had taken root in her chest.

[story 2]

Mark stared at the envelope with a furrowed brow, the crisp white paper mocking him. Inside, there was a card, clearly intended for someone else, bearing a cheerful, looping message wishing a birthday well. He picked it up, his thumb tracing the embossed lettering. A knot tightened in his stomach, a sudden, sharp clenching sensation that made his jaw ache. He felt an intense urge to simply throw the entire thing into the nearest trash receptacle. He stood by the front door for a full minute, his shoulders hunched inward as if trying to physically shrink away from the intrusion. When he finally retreated to the living room, he paced back and forth, the sound of his own footsteps echoing loudly in the quiet space, unable to settle into anything comfortable.

[story 3]

Sarah found the card tucked into the slot of her mailbox. It was an unsolicited birthday greeting, perfectly formed, addressed to an unknown recipient. When she unfolded it, the sheer anonymity of the gesture struck her with unexpected force. A wave of intense awkwardness washed over her, leaving her momentarily breathless. She stared at the handwriting, trying to decipher any clue as to who it was for, but there was nothing. Her mouth went dry. She folded the card with unnecessary precision, her movements jerky and quick. She retreated to her bedroom, leaning against the cool plaster wall, trying to regulate the rapid fluttering beneath her ribs, wishing the floor would just swallow her whole.
